headline: CATTLE CLIMB TOGETHER, OIL CRASHES 3.6%
subheadline: Live and feeder cattle both gain 0.6% as complex narrows, crude craters on Iran strike response.
lead: Live cattle closed $240.98, feeders $352.00, both up 0.6% and tracking together for the first time in six sessions. The 34-point spread that split the complex wide is finally compressing as May Cattle on Feed showed 180-day cattle at series highs, confirming the placement math that's been driving the divergence. Oil crashed 3.6% to $93.08 as fresh U.S. strikes on Iranian missile sites complicated the Hormuz deal, sending energy money flooding back into renewables and hitting input costs across the Belt.
the_takeaway: Cattle complex converging suggests the fundamental split is healing, not widening.
teaser: Cattle complex narrows for first time in a week while oil craters on Iran strikes.

one_number.unit: cattle on feed duration, series high
one_number.context: May Cattle on Feed showed cattle on feed for 180 days or more hit the highest level since the data series began. That's the placement bottleneck that's been driving live cattle higher while keeping feeders soft. When the long-fed cattle finally clear, feeder demand returns.

yesterdays_call.summary: Complex splitting wider suggests live cattle found real money, feeders still broken.
yesterdays_call.note: The complex actually narrowed today as both contracts gained equally, contradicting the widening thesis.

sections.title: Cattle Complex Converges
sections.body: Live cattle closed $240.98, up 0.6%, while feeders gained the same 0.6% to $352.00, the first day in six sessions both contracts moved in lockstep. The **34-point spread** that defined last week's divergence is finally compressing as the fundamental driver becomes clearer. May Cattle on Feed confirmed cattle on feed for 180 days or more hit series highs, the placement bottleneck that's been supporting live while pressuring feeders. When these long-fed cattle clear the system, feeder demand returns and the spread normalizes.
sections.catalyst: USDA Cattle on Feed shows 180-day cattle at series record high
sections.bottom_line: Complex healing suggests placement math working through system as expected.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: split narrowed instead of widening, countering yesterday's divergence call.

sections.title: Grains Drift Lower
sections.body: Corn closed $4.60, down 3½ cents, while beans eased 5¾ cents to $11.91 with no fund flow to speak of. The **planting calendar** is running ahead of the weather premium and managed money isn't adding conviction into Memorial Day weekend. December corn at $4.85 held the 25-cent carry to July, suggesting old-crop demand is patient, not urgent. Nebraska drought reports and Missouri replant talk aren't moving the needle yet, the market's waiting for June weather to matter.
sections.catalyst: Replant concerns in Missouri Bootheel, drought stress in western Nebraska wheat
sections.bottom_line: Weather talk building but funds waiting for June to add conviction.

sections.title: Oil Craters On Iran Strikes
sections.body: Crude crashed 3.6% to $93.08 as fresh U.S. strikes on Iranian missile sites overnight sent the **Iran premium** into reverse. The strikes complicated diplomatic progress that had been building toward a Hormuz reopening, but the market's reading it as escalation fatigue, not escalation risk. Brent gained while WTI fell, an unusual divergence that suggests the Strait closure is pricing out faster than domestic refining capacity can absorb. Energy money is rotating back into renewables as the Hormuz shutdown extends force majeure through August.
sections.catalyst: Fresh U.S. strikes on Iranian missile sites and boats overnight
sections.bottom_line: Iran premium deflating as market prices escalation fatigue over extension risk.

outside_the_pit.title: USMCA Review Could Reshape North American Ag Trade
outside_the_pit.body: An Ohio State economist warns U.S. agriculture should watch the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement review closely, with Mexico as the top ag export destination. The review timing coincides with new tariff tensions, potentially reshaping grain and livestock flows across the continent.

outside_the_pit.title: Standalone E15 Bill Has Senate Votes To Pass, Boozman Says
outside_the_pit.body: Senate Ag Committee Chairman John Boozman claims enough support for year-round E15 legislation to clear the upper chamber. If accurate, it would add 2-3 cents per bushel to corn demand during summer driving months when ethanol typically sees seasonal pressure.

outside_the_pit.title: Virtual Fencing Company Raises $10M For U.S. Expansion
outside_the_pit.body: Monil established a Kansas City subsidiary and is shipping virtual fencing products to American ranchers after raising $10 million. The technology could reduce labor costs and improve grazing management, particularly relevant as cattle prices hit multi-decade highs.

spread_to_watch.label: Live cattle / feeder cattle spread
spread_to_watch.commentary: The 34-point feeder discount to live cattle is finally compressing as the placement bottleneck shows up in Cattle on Feed data. When 180-day cattle clear the system, this spread normalizes to the seasonal $95-100 range and feeder strength returns.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt firming, Western soft on energy costs
basis.body: **Eastern Corn Belt basis** is tightening as ethanol plants restart after maintenance and rail costs ease with oil's decline. Western Belt staying soft as fertilizer application costs remain elevated despite crude's crash. The energy arbitrage is working east of the Mississippi, struggling west of it.

weekly_thread.question: Will the cattle complex split resolve through feeder strength or live cattle weakness?
weekly_thread.status_text: The split is narrowing through convergence, not breakdown. Both contracts gained 0.6% today, compressing the spread as placement data explains the divergence.

the_more_you_know.title: Series highs: when data breaks its own scale
the_more_you_know.body: Today's Cattle on Feed showed 180-day cattle at the highest level since the data series began, a 'series high' that tells you the market has never seen this placement pattern before. When a USDA series hits a record, it usually signals structural change, not seasonal variation. The 180-day number explains why live cattle keep finding buyers while feeders stay soft: the system is clogged with long-fed cattle that packers eventually have to clear. Series highs in placement data often mark turning points, not permanent conditions.

watch_list.desc: Weekly export sales; corn under 500K MT keeps funds on sidelines
Cattle above $241 confirms breakout from consolidation range
Memorial Day weekend positioning; thin trade amplifies any Iran news

daily_quote.text: The discovery of agriculture was the first big step toward a civilized life.

source_summary: USDA Cattle on Feed, CME settlement data, overnight crude futures
